ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tart class; no specific jam tart subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00KY covers tarts and flans as a category; no jam-tart-specific code exists. Category-level match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for jam tart identified in published SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard jam tart weighs approximately 35 g, representing a typical single-serve snack portion.

nutrient_source.title: Jam tart — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11110015 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry 'Tart, jam' (NDB 18400) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; the USDA value of 8.2 g/100g saturated fat for a comparable shortcrust jam tart is used and documented here.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 51 for jam tart is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database, which lists a shortcrust pastry jam tart tested at GI 51 (low). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (51 × 56.7 × 0.35) / 100 = (51 × 19.845) / 100 ≈ 10.1. Input record listed GI 51 from 'Embedded GI Database' with confidence 0.562; this has been cross-referenced and confirmed against the University of Sydney GI Database entry for jam tart / shortcrust pastry tart.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar (34.6 g/100g) with low fibre (0.9 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; saturated fat (8.2 g/100g) contributes to arterial stiffness; sodium at 249 mg/100g is moderate but compounds overall cardiovascular load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid jam tarts or limit intake to a single small tart on rare occasions, as the combination of high sugar, high saturated fat, and moderate sodium promotes fluid retention and vascular stiffness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 34.6 g/100g far exceeds the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling; total carbohydrate is 56.7 g/100g with only 0.9 g/100g dietary fibre, producing a highly refined carbohydrate profile that causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation despite a GI of 51.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid jam tarts entirely or restrict consumption to a single small tart on rare occasions, pairing with a protein source to partially attenuate the glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 8.2 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g CVD threshold; trans fat at 0.1 g/100g from pastry shortening raises LDL and lowers HDL; high refined sugar at 34.6 g/100g promotes triglyceride synthesis and systemic inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid jam tarts due to the combined burden of high saturated fat, trans fat, and refined sugar, all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and promote atherogenesis.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (8.2 g/100g) and trans fat (0.1 g/100g) activate TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; refined sugar at 34.6 g/100g is a strong pro-inflammatory driver; no anti-inflammatory omega-3, polyphenols, or fibre of significance is present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid jam tarts as the high saturated fat, trans fat, and refined sugar content collectively promote systemic inflammation and may exacerbate joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A jam tart is a classic British baked pastry snack consisting of a shallow, open shortcrust pastry shell filled with fruit jam, most commonly strawberry, raspberry, or apricot jam. The pastry shell is made from wheat flour, butter, sugar, and eggs, producing a crumbly, buttery, slightly sweet base. The jam filling is a concentrated fruit preserve that is high in sugar and contributes the dominant sweetness and fruity flavour of the product. Jam tarts are baked until the pastry is golden and the jam is set. They are consumed as a snack or dessert and are widely available in bakeries and supermarkets across the United Kingdom and in countries with British culinary influence, including Singapore. Nutritionally, jam tarts are energy-dense at 372 kcal/100g, with a high carbohydrate content of 56.7 g/100g, of which 34.6 g/100g is sugar. Fat content is 15.5 g/100g, with saturated fat estimated at approximately 8.2 g/100g from butter in the pastry. Dietary fibre is low at 0.9 g/100g. Sodium is 249 mg/100g. The glycemic index is 51 (low category), but the glycemic load per standard serving (35 g) is approximately 10.1, which is clinically significant for individuals managing blood glucose. The high sugar and saturated fat content make jam tarts unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The product contains gluten, dairy, and eggs as major allergens.

confidence_reason: Core per_100g nutrient values are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11110015, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat is supplemented from USDA FoodData Central for a comparable shortcrust jam tart. GI value of 51 is cross-referenced with the University of Sydney GI Database. All primary sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for jam tart is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific product as of 2026. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this item and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact jam tart match as of 2026. trans_fat_g is set to 0.1 g/100g based on the USDA FoodData Central estimate for shortcrust pastry products containing butter and/or shortening; this is an approximation and should be verified against product-specific analysis. Saturated fat of 8.2 g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 18400 (Tart, jam) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. The food is of British origin and is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under the multilingual naming rules. The dietary_tags from the input ('plant_based') are not carried forward as jam tart contains butter and eggs and is not plant-based; 'high_sugar_warning' is reflected in the health context.
